title: Creating Our Own Biographies and Autobiographies
explanation_chunks: Here, you'll become a real life-story writer! You'll start by interviewing a partner, asking them about their life up to age ten. Think about where they were born, who lived with them, their favourite toys, and school memories.

When you interview, it's helpful to take notes using just keywords, not full sentences, so you can quickly capture the important information. Remember to present your answers clearly, putting the most important details first.

After your interview, you'll use the information to write a short biography about your partner. You'll write a first version, called a draft, and then make it better by checking for correct spelling, good grammar (like using semi-colons and colons), and including direct speech.

You'll also get to practice switching perspectives. You'll read a piece written as an autobiography (first person) and then try rewriting it as a biography (third person), which is a clever trick for any writer to learn!
examples: When interviewing your partner, you might ask: 'What was your favourite game when you were five?' or 'Who was your first best friend?'
For a biography about your partner, you could write: 'Maya was born in Kuala Lumpur and lived with her parents and grandma.'
An example of direct speech in a biography: 'Her teacher often told her, "You are a very talented dancer."
key_points: Practice interviewing skills to gather biographical information.
Learn to organize information logically, starting with key details.
Draft and redraft a biography, focusing on standard English, correct spelling, and punctuation (semi-colons, colons, direct speech).
Understand and apply the difference between first-person (autobiography) and third-person (biography) narration.
Develop creative writing skills by continuing a life story with invented details.

vocabulary.definition: To ask questions of a person to get information from them.
The way in which something is built, arranged, or organized.
A first version of a piece of writing, not yet final.
To revise and rewrite a draft, making improvements.
A punctuation mark (;) used to join two independent clauses that are closely related.
A punctuation mark (:) used to introduce a list, an explanation, or a quote.
A short description or title of a picture or illustration.
A story or an account of events.

explain_again: This page is like being a detective! You'll ask a friend about their childhood, then write a short story (a biography) about them. You'll also learn to change a story someone wrote about themselves into a story you write about them. It's all about making your writing clear and correct!
fun_analogy: Imagine you're building with LEGOs. First, you gather the bricks (interview). Then you build a rough model (draft). Then you make it look perfect and add details (redrafting and punctuation). And sometimes, you take someone else's model and rebuild it in your own style!
